sentences.text: Yeah.
Today's date is September 20, 1995.
I'm Doris Lazarus.
I'm conducting an interview with Pincus Weinstock in his home in Bayside, Wisconsin.
Please tell us your name.
Pincus Weinstock.
P-I-N-CU-S, W-E-I-N-S-T-O-C-K.
Pincus, the same-- with a K, was it?
P-I-N-K-U-S, W-A-Y-N-S-T-R-C-K.
No.
[NON-ENGLISH]
December 6, 1915.
Wolbrom, Poland.
A smaller city.
Not too many.
Like a small city.
In a house.
We didn't have apartments in the city.
It was not modern.
You had to go for water, wintertime, bring the water in the house.
It was cold as always.
No.
A lamp.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] called it.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
I don't know what they call it, something.
My parents.
We've got five brothers.
I'm the middle one.
And the oldest is our sister.
My sister, Ruchel, was born first.
My oldest brother, Leo, Leibish, Mendel, Pincha, Motel, Yitzrik.
Yitzrik still lives in Israel.
Ruchel.
She was married.
She's got two children, a girl and a boy.
No.
Yeah, she went to Auschwitz.
With her children.
The two oldest brothers, they came out of the camp.
And one brother-- the youngest and me-- he was not in a camp.
He would have survived, I'm sure.
Now, he found out they took me to the concentration camp.
Now, he was in a good camp.
He could come home for a day.
So he came home the same day-- the evening was when they took all the Jews from the ghetto, took  them to Auschwitz.
And he went to Auschwitz.
Motel.
Schlomo, Gittel.
Mother's Gittel.
He went to markets and sold some stuff.
A market, yeah.
Clothes for suits, for all kids of material, new stuff.
No, a hard living, very hard.
Yeah.
She helped Father.
In the house?
No, not in the house.
We worked in places, we made a little money, but not in the house.
We just got one room.
One room.
Yeah, we got along, yeah.
Well, the oldest brother, right away, when they got out, maybe 15 years, they went in another city.
They started working.
The second one, the same thing.
Me, too-- later I went out, too.
A Hebrew school, not the special, good school.
My father didn't let me go.
And the regular school, he was worried I'd have to go without a cap.
So he was so religious, he didn't let us go in the school.
He was very orthodox.
Right.
I just went to, when I was 13, they took me to a temple and they gave me [NON-ENGLISH].
They called me to the Torah.
That was the whole thing.
No.
Almost every day, my father sent to shul.
Shabbat dinner.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah, like fish and everything.
Not gefilte fish, regular fish.
Tzimmes.
Yeah.
My mother made challah, yeah.
The city we was living, you couldn't buy a challah.
You have to make it for yourself.
No, just the family-- our family.
We didn't have a big home.
We couldn't invite people.
Yeah.
The city Sosnowiec.
Sosnowiec, I would say on mileage, it was about 40 miles-- kilometers, maybe 50, over 50.
By bus.
Sometimes with a horse and wagon, too.
Yeah.
It took us all night to go.
Yeah.
Sosnowiec.
They worked in chocolate-- bakery, chocolate.
Yes.
It's a Yiddish organization, Betar.
The Betar.
Yeah, Jabotinsky.
Yeah, we played baseball.
We played soccer.
Well, we didn't think too much.
We couldn't afford-- it was very hard.
If somebody wants to be something, going to college was not easy.
To be a professional was very hard.
So we didn't think about it.
We didn't have the experience, the aspirations.
Cheder.
It was not much, cheder.
In Poland, I was working, and I was doing business myself.
I made good money.
I worked in a place where they sold porcelain, glasses, pots.
So later, I went on my own.
Oh, yeah.
Very anti-Semitic, very bad, in Poland especially.
I remember they give out a paper called-- I don't know what they called the paper-- against Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember in 1936, when the president in Poland died, he was very good-- his name was Pilsudski.
He was very good for the Jews.
And the Polacks said later, your grandpa died.
So it meant they could do now what they wanted.
I think much came later from Germany. 1933, I think, the stock market got worse and worse.
What do you mean changed?
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]?
It was always anti-Semitic.
Maybe it was not so much, later it would get worse.
Oh, I remember.
The Polacks-- Polish people helped to find the Jews that were hiding.
They told the Gestapo, the Germans.
No, this was later, not '39.
Later, when the Jews started knowing what's going on, they started hiding.
I think for half a pound, for a kilogram of sugar, they said the Jews are hiding.
It was very bad.
When the war started, we ran away to another city, to Wolbrom.
Because I was in Sosnowiec when the war started.
So we were thinking-- people said it'll take a longer time for the Germans to come so far.
Meanwhile, they come faster than we went.
Sosnowiec when the war broke out.
With our parents.
Yeah, they all moved.
Well, I got a job and not far away.
From five miles of Sosnowiec is Katowice.
Katowice used to belong once, before the First World War, to Germany.
Still with my family, yeah.
No, he was a sick man.
No.
He didn't care what happened, so he didn't work.
My brothers worked.
We took every day to work.
In Katowice was a big museum, a new museum.
They took it apart.
They broke  it.
Every stone, they took out.
It took years just to do it.
[NON-ENGLISH], all kinds of things.
Yeah.
It was a foreman, a Jewish foreman.
So we have to be there every morning and go and come back.
No, this was not forced labor.
If not-- my youngest brother, 1939, or beginning of '40, they took him right away to the concentration camp.
They said, we have five brothers, one's got to go.
See, it was a Jewish-- and the police, they said, if one goes to the concentration camp, the rest will be safe.
So it went on and on-- and later, the second one-- was just to hold the people.
Well, he was the youngest, so he wants to go.
He was 17 years old, I think.
To Germany.
No.
He later went to the concentration camp, when the Judenlager, he went to the concentration camp.
He was five years.
He lives in Israel right now.
He did survive.
In the beginning, I also sent packages to the camp with food.
Yeah.
You know, it was hard to send money sometimes.
My father was smart.
He told me to take a piece of soap-- soap that you wash your hand with-- you make a hole and put some money inside, paper money, and lay the cover back.
Not a factory, just a place.
Yeah, in Katowice.
Yeah.
In Sosnowiec.
Also, they took me once-- for three months-- they took me to Breslau.
Because the Jewish people, that they send them out to Auschwitz, they have to take out all the furniture, all the food and everything.
We worked maybe three months over there.
Later, we came back to Sosnowiec.
Well, the people-- they sent them away to Auschwitz, the Jewish people, in Breslau.
So they left everything in the house-- the food and the furniture, the clothes.
So we have to move it out, everything, in trucks.
Oh, at this time, I knew of it.
In the beginning, we didn't know.
Later we know.
Maybe for three months.
Back home to Sosnowiec, to the ghetto.
It was already a ghetto.
The ghetto was Srodula.
And I went back, and I went, every day, to work, again, back to Katowice.
Yeah.
Srodula.
Yeah.
Two families, three families in one room.
Very bad, couldn't be worse.
Very hard to get food.
So if I worked, I helped something.
I brought home something, so I helped.
With permission, I think.
You have to have something, yeah.
You don't want to go-- you don't want to go out, even.
You always worry.
You have to wear a Jude arm band when you're Jewish.
The name was Merin, Jewish, I think, yeah.
There was police-- Jewish police, too, over there.
Yeah.
To the camp?
Yeah, they took out to the camp, to Auschwitz.
When the people went in this ghetto, they didn't take as many more to Auschwitz.
Before, they took plenty of people.
Not long, maybe two months, something.
Later, when I was in Katowice like I told you, I went every day.
And they took all the boys that worked over there.
They sent them-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH].
They took it, that place.
And they sent us away to Kaufering, to the concentration camp-- to Judenlager in the beginning.
The year was maybe end of '41 or '42 beginning, something like this.
No, nobody in the family.
Soon, my brother found out.
I was the last one at home.
And my brother found out they sent me away.
And he was at a good camp.
So he came home to see the parents.
The same day was-- they took all the Jews and he went too.
Yeah, we have to work every day.
All kinds of work.
I mean, to make insulation or something.
All kind-- what they need.
Every day, you didn't have the same work.
Most of the work was that they took you in a place where they cement, the things from the cement ate you up.
You couldn't work long over there.
Very bad.
Very bad.
Very bad.
They gave us not enough food.
In the beginning, I got somebody, a girl I knew of from home, she worked in the kitchen.
And then, concentration camp, the Judenlager, where she helped them out once in a while for a little bit of food.
Yeah.
In Judenlager, you could wear your clothes.
In concentration camps you got stripes.
In which camp?
In Juden-- I think a year, maybe.
I was healthy in the beginning.
But later, I was really weak.
Later, they took out the people in the Jewish concentration camp.
So they make a concentration camp.
He went in a concentration-- In the beginning, I was feeling better.
A couple days-- a couple weeks we didn't have to work.
We were just there.
So it was better.
But later it got worse and worse and worse.
This camp?
This was Dyhernfurth, Breslau.
They took us.
I was wearing-- when I was working in Dyhernfurth in concentration camp-- I was wearing shoes, Holland shoes, from wood.
So if you didn't have enough food to eat, your feet-- I got swollen up.
I figure, I have no choice.
There's two ways.
All I'll be-- if I not going over there, it just would kill me.
I would not be able to work.
The other chance I got, if I go to the [NON-ENGLISH], they will mark my number that I got on my hand.
After a couple of months, so many people goes to [NON-ENGLISH], they mark down-- write your number.
When they got enough people, they take them away to the crematorium.
I knew this before, but I had no choice.
See, this time-- if you're in a situation like this, you don't know what to do.
So I decided I go in the [NON-ENGLISH].
I went in the [NON-ENGLISH], the little hospital. 10 days I was there.
I came out a new person.
I  was able to work and everything.
So I came out.
They took my number.
I know what they marked down.
After two, three months later, they called my number out.
They didn't call us by names, just by the number.
Tomorrow morning, you have to be-- there's this place, upper place-- the trucks will be there, and you have to go away with the trucks.
Tomorrow morning, in the morning I went down.
I came down.
There were other people before that went to work at 6 o'clock in the morning.
They told me I should give them the jacket and the things that I eat.
The part, I don't know what they called it.
They know when I go, so I didn't want to get in is all.
They took me about an hour later.
We went through two days with traveling cattle.
And the cattle trucks nothing and the railroad.
And we came out, after two days, to Auschwitz.
And I got a friend, I know him good from my city, Sosnowiec.
And I know he was not able to work.
So I started taking him by the hand to work, to the truck.
And I started working with him.
He came to me, an SS man.
He said to me, go back.
Let him go by himself.
He didn't tell me more.
So I went.
I listened to what they told me.
We left 45 people. 180 were in this transport.
The rest went with the trucks.
After a half hour, they told us they should go march to a place we didn't know, but later found out this was a crematorium in the shower place-- to take a shower.
So we were there.
We were sitting, because it was summer, on the floor.
I was looking good-- I mean, feeling good, not bad.
And came an SS man again.
He told us, take off the shirts.
And he looked at us.
One he send here, one he sent here.
So out of these 45 people, left 17.
I was in the lucky of 17.
The rest went to the crematorium.
I found out when I was taken to take a shower.
I met that guy that I know from my city, Sosnowiec.
He was my barber, even.
He told me, Pincus, you've been the luckiest one.
The other ones are not alive anymore.
This was in Auschwitz.
No.
I got a uniform.
I came with a uniform.
Yeah, they always cut the hair.
I was in a camp.
They cut me two inches with a razor.
In case I run away, people should recognize me, at one camp.
You couldn't run away in Auschwitz.
Yeah, they put a number.
In Auschwitz, yeah.
'44-- The beginning of '44.
